Brett, here's a tip: Whatever you do, just don't say, "Interesting."

Instead of uttering words that contribute nothing -- in fact, let your listeners judge for themselves -- expand on the guest's answer. Take it a step further! At the very least, you can just repeat it back, in a slightly different way, to further, advance, or clarify the conversation. If you can't think of something, just parrot back. 

You can also stay silent, pause for a moment, and give your guest a chance to expand, give your listeners a chance to think and reflect on the implications of what was just said. Silence is powerful, underused, underestimated in the podcast/interviewing world. You don't need to fill every gap with some sound or remark.


Whatever you do, just don't say, "Interesting."

"Interesting" doesn't deepen the plot, elicit more story, or make your listeners any more engaged.

 It's wind. It's a waste of time. It's bland. OF COURSE the content is interesting, that's why it's in the video!

 Everyone and her dog listening to the podcast can understand what's interesting, so do something else to add to the conversation instead of stating the obvious and filling up time with fluff. ðŸ’¨

A lot of people take offense to the bath scenes, some are accusing them of sexualizing children, but it's not even that. It has nothing to do with the "sexuality" of the recording. It's the privacy of it. Nothing that happens in a bathroom should be recorded without informed consent, which, as we know, children can't give. It has the same level of violation as watching someone's doctor's appointment or sleeping. It's not sexual, it's private. There's a difference, yes. But they should both be respected. Children who aren't old enough to say "yes" should always be assumed to give a no when it comes to private things in their lives being shared, recorded, or documented. A parent that takes advantage of their legal power and authority over a child to force them to say yes should be investigated and at worst, imprisoned. 

It doesn't matter how "bad" it truly is for them. She is violating them and their inability to say "no." They could be perfectly healthy and happy children who grow up to say they loved filming these and it would still be wrong because of the possibility of the alternative. There is no way to know if they would be okay with this given full context. There is no way to know the effect or damage doing something like this could cause. Even if they simply feel slightly embarrassed by having these videos out there, that's enough for me to consider this a crime. They are under her complete control and authority. She is using them like props for her monetary gain before they are old enough to fully grasp what's happening to them. 

"It's not that bad." minimizes what is truly going on here. Abuse isn't always hitting, beating, and yelling. It isn't always neglect. Sometimes, abuse is silent. Don't let the silence deafen you.
